preamble: The transformation of the voiced velar fricative [ɣ] into the close back rounded vowel [u] is a documented historical sound change, notably occurring in the development of Old English into Middle English. This process, often termed vocalization, involves a consonant becoming more vowel-like, typically in specific phonetic environments.
phoneticEffects: This shift represents a vocalization, a form of lenition where a fricative consonant loses its consonantal stricture and becomes a vowel

examples.note: The Old English voiced velar fricative /ɣ/ in 'sagu' vocalized to /u/ in the diphthong of Middle English 'sawe' after a back vowel.
The Old English voiced velar fricative /ɣ/ in 'boga' vocalized to /u/ in the diphthong of Middle English 'bowe' after a back vowel.
The Old English voiced velar fricative /ɣ/ in 'lagu' vocalized to /u/ in the diphthong of Middle English 'lawe'.

sources: Algeo, J., & Pyles, T. (2010). The Origins and Development of the English Language (6th ed.). Cengage.
